content.overview:
Rubella is a highly contagious disease caused by a virus. It spreads easily when an infected person coughs or sneezes. Most children and adults who get rubella have a mild fever and rash.

When a woman is infected with the rubella virus early in pregnancy, she has a 90% chance of passing the virus on to her fetus. Rubella in pregnancy, especially during the first trimester, can result in miscarriage, fetal death, stillbirth, or infants with congenital malformations, known as congenital rubella syndrome (CRS).

Being vaccinated is the best way to prevent getting sick with rubella or spreading it to other people. The vaccine is safe and helps your body fight off the virus.

- Rubella is a contagious viral infection transmitted by airborne droplets that occurs most often in children and young adults.
- Rubella is the leading vaccine-preventable cause of birth defects, accounting for an estimated 100 000 infants born with congenital rubella syndrome (CRS) each year worldwide.
- Rubella virus infection during pregnancy may result in miscarriage, fetal death or CRS. The most severe damage occurs early in pregnancy, especially in the first trimester.
- Even though a safe and cost-effective vaccine is available, in 2022, there were an estimated 17 865 cases of rubella in 78 countries.

--- IMPORTANT SAFETY NOTE ---
This file is for health awareness and RAG knowledge-base use only. It is not medical advice, diagnosis, prescription, or treatment protocol. Always consult a qualified healthcare professional for personal medical decisions or emergencies.
types: When a woman is infected with the rubella virus early in pregnancy, she has a 90% chance of passing the virus on to her fetus. Rubella in pregnancy, especially during the first trimester, can result in miscarriage, fetal death, stillbirth, or infants with congenital malformations, known as congenital rubella syndrome (CRS).

Rubella vaccines are available either in monovalent formulation (a vaccine directed at only one pathogen) or more commonly in combinations with other vaccines such as with vaccines against measles (MR), measles and mumps (MMR), or measles, mumps and varicella (MMRV).
symptoms: In children, the disease is usually mild, with symptoms including a rash, low fever (<39°C), nausea and mild conjunctivitis. The rash, which occurs in 50–80% of cases, lasts 1–3 days and usually starts on the face and neck before progressing down the body. Swollen lymph glands behind the ears and in the neck are the most characteristic clinical feature. Infected adults, more commonly women, may develop arthritis and painful joints that usually last from 3–10 days.

Once a person is infected, the virus spreads throughout the body in about 5–7 days. Symptoms usually appear 2 to 3 weeks after exposure. The most infectious period is usually 1–5 days after the appearance of the rash.

When a woman is infected with the rubella virus early in pregnancy, she has a 90% chance of passing the virus on to her fetus. This can cause the death of the fetus, or it may cause CRS. Infants with CRS may excrete the virus for a year or more.
warning_signs: Rubella virus infection during pregnancy may result in miscarriage, fetal death or CRS. The most severe damage occurs early in pregnancy, especially in the first trimester.

When a woman is infected with the rubella virus early in pregnancy, she has a 90% chance of passing the virus on to her fetus. Rubella in pregnancy, especially during the first trimester, can result in miscarriage, fetal death, stillbirth, or infants with congenital malformations, known as congenital rubella syndrome (CRS).

When a woman is infected with the rubella virus early in pregnancy, she has a 90% chance of passing the virus on to her fetus. This can cause the death of the fetus, or it may cause CRS. Infants with CRS may excrete the virus for a year or more.

M&RP is part of IA2030 , an ambitious global strategy to maximize the lifesaving impact of vaccines – one of the most successful and cost-effective public health interventions of all time. IA2030 aims to avert over 50 million deaths by 2030 through access to essential vaccines and help build healthcare systems that can withstand the impact of pandemics and deliver rapid vaccination response.
risk_factors: Rubella is a contagious viral infection transmitted by airborne droplets that occurs most often in children and young adults.
Rubella is a highly contagious disease caused by a virus. It spreads easily when an infected person coughs or sneezes. Most children and adults who get rubella have a mild fever and rash.

Once a person is infected, the virus spreads throughout the body in about 5–7 days. Symptoms usually appear 2 to 3 weeks after exposure. The most infectious period is usually 1–5 days after the appearance of the rash.

The highest risk of CRS is in countries where women of childbearing age do not have immunity to the disease (either through vaccination or from having had rubella). Before the introduction of the vaccine, up to 4 babies in every 1000 live births were born with CRS.
prevention: Rubella is the leading vaccine-preventable cause of birth defects, accounting for an estimated 100 000 infants born with congenital rubella syndrome (CRS) each year worldwide.
Even though a safe and cost-effective vaccine is available, in 2022, there were an estimated 17 865 cases of rubella in 78 countries.
Being vaccinated is the best way to prevent getting sick with rubella or spreading it to other people. The vaccine is safe and helps your body fight off the virus.
There were an estimated 17 865 cases of rubella in 78 countries in 2022, despite the availability of a safe and cost-effective vaccine.

The Measles & Rubella Partnership (M&RP), previously the Measles & Rubella Initiative, has been helping deliver measles and rubella vaccines since 2000 to children worldwide, contributing to saving an estimated 57 million lives and substantially reducing birth defects due to congenital rubella infections.

The highest risk of CRS is in countries where women of childbearing age do not have immunity to the disease (either through vaccination or from having had rubella). Before the introduction of the vaccine, up to 4 babies in every 1000 live births were born with CRS.
environmental_control: Once a person is infected, the virus spreads throughout the body in about 5–7 days. Symptoms usually appear 2 to 3 weeks after exposure. The most infectious period is usually 1–5 days after the appearance of the rash.
vaccination: The rubella vaccine is a live attenuated strain, and a single dose gives more than 95% long-lasting immunity, which is similar to that induced by natural infection.

Rubella vaccines are available either in monovalent formulation (a vaccine directed at only one pathogen) or more commonly in combinations with other vaccines such as with vaccines against measles (MR), measles and mumps (MMR), or measles, mumps and varicella (MMRV).

Adverse reactions following vaccination are generally mild. They may include pain and redness at the injection site, low-grade fever, rash and muscle aches. Mass immunization campaigns in the Region of the Americas involving more than 250 million adolescents and adults did not identify any serious adverse reactions associated with the vaccine.
diagnosis: Diagnosis should be made by a qualified healthcare professional using appropriate history, examination, and tests where needed.
treatment_awareness_only: Children with CRS can suffer hearing impairments, eye and heart defects and other lifelong disabilities, including autism, diabetes mellitus and thyroid dysfunction – many of which require costly therapy, surgeries and other expensive care.

M&RP is part of IA2030 , an ambitious global strategy to maximize the lifesaving impact of vaccines – one of the most successful and cost-effective public health interventions of all time. IA2030 aims to avert over 50 million deaths by 2030 through access to essential vaccines and help build healthcare systems that can withstand the impact of pandemics and deliver rapid vaccination response.
when_to_see_a_doctor: Consult a qualified healthcare professional if symptoms are severe, persistent, recurrent, worsening, or affecting daily life.

Seek urgent care for red-flag symptoms such as breathing difficulty, chest pain, heavy bleeding, severe dehydration, confusion, fainting, seizures, pregnancy danger signs, suspected poisoning/exposure, or signs of severe infection.

For prevention, screening, vaccination, sexual/reproductive health, child health, mental health, disability support, or chronic-condition concerns, use a government health facility, PHC/CHC, district hospital, or registered clinician.
india_specific_context: This entry is based primarily on WHO global guidance. In India, confirm current national or state guidance through MoHFW, NHM, NHP, ICMR/NCDC, FSSAI, CPCB, NIMHANS, or the relevant public programme when applicable.
Government PHCs, CHCs, district hospitals, ASHA/ANM workers, and emergency number 112 can guide access to appropriate services.

Laws and service availability for reproductive health, disability benefits, insurance, environmental exposure control, mental health support, and public-health reporting can vary by state and may change; verify locally.

faqs.question: Is rubella still a risk in India?
faqs.answer: Yes. Despite significant progress, rubella cases and congenital rubella syndrome (CRS) still occur in India. The Measles-Rubella (MR) vaccination campaign has dramatically reduced cases, but maintaining high vaccination coverage is essential.

faqs.question: Why is rubella especially dangerous during pregnancy?
faqs.answer: Rubella infection in the first trimester of pregnancy causes Congenital Rubella Syndrome (CRS) in up to 90% of cases — causing deafness, blindness, heart defects, and intellectual disability in the baby. This is preventable with MR vaccination before pregnancy.

faqs.question: Is the rubella vaccine free in India?
faqs.answer: Yes. Measles-Rubella (MR) vaccine is free under India's Universal Immunisation Programme, given at 9 and 15 months. National MR campaigns have vaccinated hundreds of millions of children. Women of childbearing age who are not immune should receive the vaccine before getting pregnant (avoid in pregnancy).

faqs.question: How do I know if I am immune to rubella before planning pregnancy?
faqs.answer: A simple blood test (rubella IgG) checks immunity. It is recommended as part of preconception screening. If not immune, get the MR vaccine and wait 1 month before trying to conceive. Free preconception check-ups are available at government hospitals.
